title: Economic methodology
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS — positive vs normative, ceteris paribus, and economic models.
learningObjectives: Describe the role of economic models.

simpleExplanation.title: How economists think
simpleExplanation.summary: Economics uses simplified models to explain behaviour. Statements must be classified as testable facts or value judgements.
steps: Positive: can be tested with data.

flashcards.back: A testable statement about what is — can be verified with evidence (e.g. "Unemployment is 5%").
A value judgement about what ought to be — contains words like should, ought, fair (e.g. "Tax should be cut").
All other things being equal — isolates the effect of one variable by holding others constant.
To simplify reality, explain behaviour, and make predictions — assumptions must be stated clearly.

flashcards.front: Give one limitation of economic models.
flashcards.back: Assumptions may not hold (e.g. rational behaviour); models omit variables and cannot capture all real-world complexity.

flashcards.front: Can normative statements be proved true or false?
flashcards.back: No — they depend on values and opinions, not data alone.

sections.content: **Economic methodology** (1.2) underpins every essay. Label statements **positive or normative** before evaluating policy.

sections.question:
Classify each statement as positive or normative:

(a) "A 10% rise in the minimum wage reduces employment among low-skilled workers."
(b) "The government ought to subsidise renewable energy."
(c) "Inflation was 3.2% last year."
sections.solution:
(a) **Positive** — a testable hypothesis about cause and effect; can be checked with employment data.

(b) **Normative** — "ought to" expresses a value judgement about desirable policy.

(c) **Positive** — a factual claim verifiable from official statistics.

**Exam tip:** In evaluation essays, use positive analysis first (what happens), then normative judgement (whether it is desirable).

steps.caption: Normative: value judgements — what ought to be.
Ceteris paribus: one variable changes, others held constant.
